interpretations.interpretation: A genealogical “proof” that Jesus belongs to the expected royal line: beginning with Abraham (founding ancestor of God’s people), continuing through David (the covenant king), and ending with “Christ,” implying legitimacy for the Messiah.

A carefully structured arrangement meant to show divine order and fulfillment, not merely a family record—three sections of fourteen generations indicating that God’s plan unfolds in a purposeful pattern.

A numerical/theological significance of “fourteen”: many listeners would understand the repetition as meaningful for signaling completion of God’s historical timeline (for example, aligning with common Jewish practices of reading significance into numbers and structured sequences).
A claim that the period of exile (“the deportation to Babylon”) is not the end of God’s work but part of a continuous story leading to the Messiah—interpreted as restoration after a crisis.
An assurance to Jewish audiences that, despite the historical break caused by the Babylonian exile, God’s covenant promises remain active and culminate in the coming of the Messiah in Jesus.

For early Christian audiences, a way to interpret Jesus’ identity through the story of Israel: the genealogy serves as a bridge from Israel’s scriptures (Abraham, David, Babylonian exile) to the new moment in Christ.
